user_input: Given the safety requirements for exception handling in safety-related software as outlined in ISO 26262-6, how does Rule A15-0-1 in the AUTOSAR standard influence the design of a function that may encounter errors during its execution?
reference: Rule A15-0-1 in the AUTOSAR standard reinforces the ISO 26262-6 recommendation against hidden control flow by prohibiting functions from exiting with exceptions unless they fail to complete their assigned tasks. This ensures that exceptions are used only for error conditions, maintaining clear and predictable control flow, which is crucial for the safety and reliability of safety-related software.
reference_contexts:
Challenges of using exceptions
Issue: Solution:
Correctness of the exception handling Exception handling mechanism is implemented by
the compiler (by its library functions and machine
code generator) and defined by the C++ Language
Standard. Rule A1-2-1 requires that the compiler
(including its exception handling routines), when
used for safety-related software, meets appropriate
safety requirements.
Hidden control flow ISO 26262-6 (T able *) recommends “no hidden
data flow or control flow” for ASIL A software and
highly recommends it for ASIL B/C/D. Therefore, the
Rule A15-0-1 prohibits the usage of exceptions for
normal control flow of software - they are allowed
only for errors where a function failed to perform its
assigned task.

6.15.0 General
Rule A15-0-1 (required, architecture / design / implementation,
non-automated)
A function shall not exit with an exception if it is able to complete its task.
Rationale
“The notion of an exception is provided to help get information from the point where an
error is detected to a point where it can be handled. A function that cannot cope with
a problem throws an exception, hoping that its (direct or indirect) caller can handle the
problem. A function that wants to handle a kind of problem indicates that by catching
the corresponding exception. ” [The C++ Programming Language [14]]
Exceptions are only supposed to be used to capture incorrect, and which is not
expected to be seen in normal program, execution. Using exception handling
mechanism to transfer control back up the call stack, in error-free situation, leads to

user_input: Given the requirement for access serialization across cores in multi-core environments, what specific measures must be taken to ensure reentrancy and concurrency safety in shared code according to AUTOSAR standards?
reference: To ensure reentrancy and concurrency safety in shared code in multi-core environments, AUTOSAR standards require that shared code must avoid the use of static and global variables, guard such variables using blocking mechanisms, and use dynamic stack variables. These measures are necessary to prevent conflicts when a single copy of the routine is called from several task contexts simultaneously, ensuring that the code remains reentrant and concurrency-safe across multiple cores.
reference_contexts:
Constraints 
To realize the concept further constraints are defined to prevent inefficient and multi-
core blocking implementations. In this sense it is especially important to consider that 
it is not sufficient anymore to implement exclusive areas on a single core but to 
additionally ensure an access serialization in case resources need to be shared 
across several partitions distributed to several cores. 
 
1.  Access Serialization on a single core: For single-core systems concurrency 
problems are well understood and mitigated by exclusive areas, which limit 
concurrent access to one process at a time. This is typically done by locking 
interrupts, employing OS resources or creating a non-pre-emptive scheduling. 
This effectively means access-serialization of the different processes. 
2.  Access Serialization across cores: Since exclusive areas only have a core-
wide scope, they are not sufficient to prevent concurrent access in multi-core 
environments. But as soon as it is required to access the same resource (e.g. 
by access to the service API’s, processing of the ISRs and main-functions) it is 
required to introduce cross-core means. Besides the usage of atomic 
resources, the worst – because blocking – one would be the introduction of a

Type: Valid 
Description: All AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules shall export only that kind of 
information in their correspondent header-files explicitly needed by other 
modules. 
Rationale: Prevent other modules accessing functionality and data that is ‘none of their 
business’.   
Use Case: The NVRAM Manager shall not know all processor registers because 
someone has included the processor register file in another header file used 
by the NVRAM manager. 
Dependencies: -- 
Supporting Material: -- 
⌋(RS_BRF_02024) 
5.2.3.5.3  [SRS_BSW_00328] All AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules shall avoid 
the duplication of code 
⌈ 
Type: Valid 
Description: All AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules should avoid the duplication of code. 
Rationale: Avoid bugs during maintenance 
Use Case: A module contains 4 code segments which are equal. During maintenance of 
the module 3 of them have been updated, 1 has been forgotten  BUG. 
Dependencies: -- 
Supporting Material: -- 
⌋(RS_BRF_02072,RS_BRF_02112,RS_BRF_02032) 
5.2.3.5.4 [SRS_BSW_00312] Shared code shall be reentrant  
⌈ 
Type: Valid 
Description: All AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules implementing shared code shall 
ensure reentrancy if code is exposed to preemptive or parallel environments. 
For multi-core systems, reentrancy shall be ensured for unrestricted 
concurrent execution of that service on several cores (concurrency safety). 
Rationale: Shared code eases functional composition, reusability, code size reduction 
and maintainability. As a drawback, shared code shall be implemented 
reentrant if it is used in preemptive environments or on multiple partitions in 
parallel. Please note that an implementation that is reentrant on single core 
systems might not be concurrency safe when used in a Multi-Core 
environment. 
Use Case: A subroutine or function is reentrant if a single copy of the routine can be 
called from several task contexts simultaneously without conflict. Use the 
following reentrancy techniques: 
 
- Avoid use of static and/or global variables 
- Guard static and/or global variables using blocking mechanisms 
- Use dynamic stack variables  
Dependencies: -- 
Supporting Material: --

user_input: How does the little-endian encoding of the E2E header fields in E2E Profile 11, as specified in the system template, affect the placement and calculation of the Data ID when DataIDMode is set to E2E_P11_DATAID_NIBBLE?
reference: In E2E Profile 11, when DataIDMode is set to E2E_P11_DATAID_NIBBLE, the lower nibble of the high byte of the DataID is placed at the bit position DataIDNibbleOffset in the transmitted data. The CRC calculation is performed by first calculating over the low byte of the DataID, followed by a 0-byte, and then the user data. The little-endian encoding ensures that the least significant byte is transmitted first, affecting the order in which the DataID and user data are processed during transmission.
reference_contexts:
E2E Protocol Specification
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0
4. E2E_P11DataIDMode = E2E_P11_DA T AID_NIBBLE
5. SignalIPdu.unusedBitPattern = 0xFF
⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
 E2E Profile variant 11C relates to Configuration of E2E Profile 11 configuration setting
11C in system template (system template is more specific).
The transmission order shown above represents the order in which bits are transmitted.
For comparability to the figures of profile 1, also the bit order is given. The E2E header
fields (e.g. CRC) are encoded like in CAN and FlexRay , i.e.:
1. Little Endian (least significant byte fist) applicable for both implicit and explicit
header fields - imposed by profile
2. MSB Fist (most significant bit within byte first) - imposed by Flexray/CAN bus.

E2E Protocol Specification
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0

calculation shall be done by first calculating over the low byte of the Data ID, then a
0-byte, and then the user data. ⌋(RS_E2E_08539)
Note: the byte containing the CRC is always omitted from the CRC calculation.

reference: The IPv6 Endpoint Option in Offer Service entries provides the server's endpoint details, including the IP address and port numbers for both TCP and UDP. user_input: How does the configuration of CrcTimeFlagsTxSecured, as specified in the Time Master's responsibilities, influence the calculation of the CRC_Time element during the validation process in the Time Secured sub-TL V?
reference: The configuration of CrcTimeFlagsTxSecured by the Time Master influences the calculation of the CRC_Time element during the validation process by determining which fields are included in the CRC calculation. Specifically, the bits in CrcTimeFlagsTxSecured indicate whether the CRCMessageLength, CRCDomainNumber, CRC_CorrectionField, CRCSourcePortIdentity, and CRCSequenceIdentity are considered in the CRC calculation. If the validation CRC is successful, the correctionField is modified, and the CRC_Time is calculated based on these flags.

user_input: How does the ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef differ from the ApmcUpstreamDocInstanceReferenceDef in terms of their ability to reference model elements on the design level and the implications for the existence of the target model elements?
reference: The ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef and ApmcUpstreamDocInstanceReferenceDef both represent the ability to establish a flat reference to model elements on the design level. However, the ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef does not require the target of the reference to exist in the same model scope, and its existence can serve as an additional identifying element of the enclosing ApmcContainerValue. In contrast, the ApmcUpstreamDocInstanceReferenceDef is used to identify the model element that sparked the existence of the owner of the reference, and its existence is considered pure metadata without carrying any additional identifying implications.
reference_contexts:
Adaptive Platform Machine Configuration
AUTOSAR AP R25-11
The difference is that in the case of the ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef the target
of the structured reference is not required to exist in the same model scope.
On the “value side”, if the target of the reference does not exist in the same model
scope, then the existence of the ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef can serve as
an additional identifying element of the enclosing ApmcContainerValue of the
ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef.⌋
 Class ApmcUriInstanceReferenceDef
Note This meta-class represents the ability to establish a flat (as opposed to an instanceRef) reference to
model elements on design level. In other words, it is possible to annotate which design element was
taken as the basis for the target-configuration model element that owns the reference. The target of the
reference is not required to exist. The reference itself is already considered an identifying element.

user_input: Given the requirement that the SOME/IP protocol supports grouping fields into eventgroups (RS_SOMEIP_00018), how does the server handle the initial events for a field when a client subscribes to multiple eventgroups of the same Service Instance that include the same field, as specified in PRS_SOMEIPSD_00571 and PRS_SOMEIPSD_00572?
reference: When a client subscribes to multiple eventgroups of the same Service Instance that include the same field, the server handles the initial events as follows: if the subscriptions are made through different SOME/IP-SD messages, the server sends out the initial events for the field separately for each subscription. However, if the subscriptions are made through the same SOME/IP-SD message, the server may choose to send the initial event for the field only once.

user_input: How does the definition of the Application ID and Context ID in the trace message structure (from Context 1) influence the implementation of the SetLogLevel service (from Context 2) in terms of controlling the log levels for specific applications and contexts?
reference: The Application ID and Context ID in the trace message structure uniquely identify the source of log and trace messages. In the SetLogLevel service, these identifiers are used as parameters to control the log levels for specific applications and contexts. If the Application ID is provided and the Context ID is NULL, the log level is set for all Context IDs associated with that Application ID. If both Application ID and Context ID are provided, the log level is set specifically for that combination. This allows fine-grained control over log levels for different parts of the software.
reference_contexts:
Trace Message A Trace messages contains information, which has passed via the VFB. 
ECU ID 
 
The ECU ID is the name of an ECU, composed by four 8-bit ASCII 
characters (e.g. ABS0 or COMB).  
FIBEX The Field Bus Exchange Format is a versatile XML bases description 
format. 
Session A session is the logical entity of the source of log or trace messages. If a 
SW-C / application is inst antiated several times , a session for each 
instance with a globally unique session ID is used. 
Session ID The Session ID is the identification number of a log or trace session 
Application ID 
 
Application ID is an abbreviation of a SW-C / application.  
It identifies the SW-C / application the log and trace message originates. 
Context ID 
 
Context ID is a user defined ID to group Log and Trace Messages 
generated by a SW-C / application. The following rules apply:  
 Each Application ID can own several Context IDs.  
 Context IDs are grouped by Application IDs.  
 Context IDs shall be unique within an Application ID. 
 The source of a log and trace message is identified using 
the tuple “Application ID” and “Context ID”.  
 
Four 8-bit ASCII characters compose the Context ID. 
Message ID 
 
Messaged ID is the ID to characterize the information, which is transported 
by the message itself. A Message ID identifies a log or trace message 
uniquely. It can be used for identifying the source (in source code) of a 
message and it can be used for characterizing the payload of a message. 
A Message ID is statically fixed at development or configuration time. 
Log level A log level defines a classification for the severity grade of a Log Message.  
Trace status The trace status provides information if a trace message should be send. 
Log Channel A physical Communications Bus which is used to transmit Dlt messages.  
External client 
 
The external client is a tool to control, monitor and store the log/trace 
information provided by the ECUs using the Dlt module.

Log and Trace Protocol Specification 
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0 
 
45 of 69 Document ID 787: AUTOSAR_PRS_LogAndTraceProtocol 
⌋ (RS_LT_00032) 
 Note: 
It is recommended that the defined Dlt Commands can be triggered by the reception 
of the corresponding Dlt Control Message, and/or via separate C APIs. 
 
 
[PRS_Dlt_00187] ⌈Control messages are normal Dlt messages with a Standard Header, 
an Extended Header, and payload. The payload contains of the Service ID and the 
contained parameters.  
 
 5.3.1 Set Log Level 
[PRS_Dlt_00194] ⌈ 
 Service name: SetLogLevel 
Service_ID [hex] 0x01 
Sync/Async: Synchronous 
Reentrancy: Non Reentrant 
 Request Parameter 
Number Name Type Description 
1 applicationId 4*uint8 Representation of the Application ID.  
If this field is filled with NULL all log level for all Context IDs 
on this ECU are set. 
2 contextId 4*uint8 Representation of the Context ID  
 If this field is filled with NULL all Context IDs belonging to 
the given Application ID are set. 
 is only interpreted if Application ID is not NULL 
3 newLogLevel sint8 the new log level to set  
 can be in the range of DLT_LOG_FATAL to 
DLT_LOG_VERBOSE for setting the pass through range 
 if set to 0 all messages from this Context ID are blocked 
 if set to -1 the default log level for this ECU will be used 
4 reserved 4*uint8 Reserved – These 4 bytes shall be ignored (i.e.: “don’t care”) 
This field shall be filled with zeros.

reference: The Time Master sends the 'second' part of the Offset Time Base value via the OfsTimeSec element and the 'nanosecond' part via the OfsTimeNSec element. The Time Slave then calculates the Offset Time Base using these specific elements, ensuring that both the seconds and nanoseconds are accurately synchronized between the Time Master and the Time Slave.

reference: The abstract base class ApmcReferenceValue serves as a foundation for representing references on the 'value' side of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. ApmcForeignReferenceValue, a concrete subclass of ApmcReferenceValue, specifically enables the establishment of references to model elements outside the target-configuration, within the design model. This relationship is defined through the inheritance hierarchy, where ApmcForeignReferenceValue inherits from ApmcReferenceValue, inheriting its properties and extending its functionality to support external references.

user_input: How does the behavior of a client upon receiving a Stop Offer Service entry, as described in the Shutdown Behavior, affect the conditions under which a client can send a Find Service entry according to the Service Entries specification?
reference: When a client receives a Stop Offer Service entry, all subscriptions for this service instance are deleted on the client side, and the client is not allowed to send out Find Service entries. Instead, the client must wait for an Offer Service entry or a change in status (such as application, network management, or Ethernet link changes) before attempting to find the service again.
reference_contexts:
Service Entries
Find Service Entry
[PRS_SOMEIPSD_00350]⌈ The Find Service entry type shall be used for finding
service instances and shall only be sent if the current state of a service is unknown
(no current Service Offer was received and is still valid). ⌋(RS_SOMEIPSD_00008,
RS_SOMEIPSD_00021)
[PRS_SOMEIPSD_00351]⌈ Find Service entries shall set the entry fields in the fol-
lowing way:
• T ype shall be set to 0x00 (FindService).
• Service ID shall be set to the Service ID of the service that shall be found.
• Instance ID shall be set to 0xFFFF , if all service instances shall be returned. It
shall be set to the Instance ID of a specific service instance, if just a single service
instance shall be returned.
• Major Version shall be set to 0xFF , that means that services with any version shall
be returned. If set to value different than 0xFF , services with this specific major
version shall be returned only .
• Minor Version shall be set to 0xFFFF FFFF , that means that services with any
version shall be returned. If set to a value different to 0xFFFF FFFF , services
with this specific minor version shall be returned only .
• TTL shall be set to the lifetime of the Find Service entry . After this lifetime the
Find Service entry shall be considered not existing.
• If TTL is set to 0xFFFFFF , the Find Service entry shall be considered valid until
the next reboot.
• TTL shall not be set to 0x000000 since this is considered to be the Stop Offer
Service Entry .

Shutdown Behavior

user_input: How does the use of a 'GetHandler' in the Communication Management implementation affect the synchronization of field values between on-change notifications and explicit Get() calls, and what are the implications for the design of the interface that aggregates methods for receiving notifications, getting, and setting field values?
reference: When a 'GetHandler' is used in the Communication Management implementation, it can lead to a discrepancy between the field values delivered via on-change notifications and those returned by explicit Get() calls. This occurs because the Communication Management does not automatically synchronize the value returned from the 'GetHandler' with the value sent through on-change notifications unless the 'GetHandler' internally calls Update() with the same value. This can result in inefficiencies, as the Communication Management must call into user space and serialize the field value for each Get() call, which could be optimized away if no 'GetHandler' is registered. Therefore, the design of the interface that aggregates methods for receiving notifications, getting, and setting field values must carefully consider the use of 'GetHandler' to ensure consistent and efficient field value management.
reference_contexts:
AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10
In this case, new subscribers will get potentially outdated field values on subscription,
since updating of the field value is deferred to the explicit call of a “GetHandler”.
Y ou also have to keep in mind: In such a setup, with enabled “on-change-notification”
together with a registered “GetHandler” the Communication Management implemen-
tation will not automatically care for, that the value the developer returns from the
“GetHandler” will be synchronized with value, which subscribers get via “on-change-
notification” event!
If the implementation of “GetHandler” does not internally call Update() with the same
value, which it will deliver back via ara:com promise, then the field value delivered via
“on-change-notification” event will differ from the value returned to the Get() call. I.e.
the Communication Management implementation will not automatically/internally call
Update() with the value the “GetHandler” returned.
Bottom line: Using RegisterGetHandler is rather an exotic use case and develop-
ers should be aware of the intrinsic effect.
Additionally a user provided “GetHandler”, which only returns the current value, which
has already been updated by the service implementation via Update(), is typically
very inefficient! The Communication Management then has to call to user space and
to additionally apply field serialization of the returned value at any incoming Get() call.
Both things could be totally “optimized away” if the developer does not register a “GetH-
andler” and leaves the handling of Get() calls entirely to the Communication Manage-
ment implementation.
6.3.6.2

5.1.2.3.3 Fields
[RS_CM_00216] Communication Management shall provide an interface which
aggregates methods to receive a notification on a changed field value as well as
explicitly getting and setting the field value ⌈
Type: draft
Description:
The interface shall aggregate the methods to receive a notification that the field
value has changed, explicitly get the field value and explicitly set the field value.
It shall also be possible to aggregate any non-empty subset of these methods.
Rationale: T o access properties held in a central location it is necessary to be able to
query and modify the value as well as get notifications on change of the value.
Dependencies: –
Use Case:
The consumers of sensor data would like to know the update rate of the sensor
data and want to be able to change this value if they have different
requirements.
Supporting
Material:
–
⌋(RS_Main_01002, RS_Main_00060)

user_input: How does the specification of the Type Length (TYLE) for standard data types in the Log and Trace Protocol affect the interpretation of the Data Payload when the Type Array (ARAY) is set?
reference: The Type Length (TYLE) for standard data types specifies the length of the data types used in the Data Payload. When the Type Array (ARAY) is set, the TYLE field is interpreted similarly to standard data types, indicating the length of the elements within the array. This ensures that each element in the n-dimensional array is correctly sized and interpreted according to its specified data type, whether it is bool, signed integer, unsigned integer, or float.
reference_contexts:
Log and Trace Protocol Specification 
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0 
 
32 of 69 Document ID 787: AUTOSAR_PRS_LogAndTraceProtocol

5.1.3.1  Bits Type Length (TYLE) 
 
 
Type Length specifies the length of the standard data type.  
 
[PRS_Dlt_00354] ⌈Type Length is a bit field of 4 bit.  
 
Type Info contains  
 1 (8 bit) for bool data (BOOL)  
 1 (8 bit) or 2 (16 bit) or 3 (32 bit)  or 4 (64 bit) or 5 (128 bit) for signed 
(SINT) and unsigned integer data (UINT) 
 2 (16 bit) or 3 (32 bit) or  4 (64 bit) or 5 (128 bit) for float data (FLOA) ⌋ 
(RS_LT_00044) 
 
 
5.1.3.2  Bit Variable Info (VARI) 
 
If Variable Info (VARI) is set, the name and the unit of a variable can be added. Both 
always contain a length information field and a field with the text (of name or unit). 
The length field contains the number of characters of the associated name or unit 
filed. The unit information is to add only in some data types.

user_input: How does the memory partitioning mechanism described in the AUTOSAR OS specification for ensuring freedom from interference between OS-Applications with different ASIL ratings impact the configuration of core-specific ASIL and QM partitions in a multi-core system?
reference: The memory partitioning mechanism in the AUTOSAR OS ensures that OS-Applications with different ASIL ratings are isolated from each other, preventing write access to private data sections and stacks. In a multi-core system, this isolation is extended to core-specific partitions, where BSW is assigned to core-specific QM and ASIL partitions. Each core has its own set of QM and ASIL partitions, and the configuration parameters for these partitions must be set independently to meet both multi-core and safety requirements, ensuring that the memory protection mechanisms are maintained across cores.
reference_contexts:
Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
13 of 96 Document ID 664: AUTOSAR_TR_OverviewOfFunctionalSafetyMeasuresInAUTOSAR 
 
2.1.2.4  Memory Partitioning within Application Software 
Application Software in an AUTOSAR ECU can consist of safety -related and non -
safety-related Software Component s.  Freedom from interference between Software 
Components with different ASIL ratings shall be ensured according to the 
requirements of ISO 262629. 
 
The AUTOSAR Operating System provides freedom from interference for memory -
related faults by placing OS -Applications into separate memory regions. This 
mechanism is called Memory Partitioning. OS -Applications are protected from each 
other, as code executing in the Memory Partition of one OS -Application cannot 
modify other memory regions. The corresponding requi rements from the AUTOSAR 
OS specification are presented in Table 1. 
 
Req. ID Requirement Text 
[SWS_Os_ 
00207] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to the OS -
Application’s private data sections from other non -trusted OS -
Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00355] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to all private 
stacks of Tasks/Category 2 ISRs of an OS -Application from other non -
trusted OS-Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00356] 
The Operating System module shall pre vent write access to all private 
data sections of a Task/Category 2 ISR of an OS -Application from other 
non-trusted OS-Applications. 
Table 1:

Guide to BSW Distribution 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
58 of 64 Document ID 631:  AUTOSAR_EXP_BSWDistributionGuide 
The feature can be enabled for each trusted OsApplication via the configuration 
parameter OsTrustedApplicationDelayTimingViolationCall. 
 
3.2.8  Combining Safety and Multi-Core 
In case ASIL systems are implemented using a multi-core architecture, all 
considerations made until now for both, safety and multi-core, are valid. In a multi-
core system, the BSW is assigned to core specific partitions. If safety is added, we 
have core specific QM partitions (one per core) and core specific ASIL partitions. The 
specific multi-core configuration parameters and the specific safety configuration 
parameters are independent and need to be set according to the multi-core 
respectively safety needs.  
 
3.2.9

user_input: Given the requirement that the Discardable Flag in the IPv4 Endpoint Option shall be set to 0, how does this interact with the rule that the Discardable Flag in the Load Balancing Option can be set to 1 if the Option can be discarded by the receiver?
reference: The Discardable Flag in the IPv4 Endpoint Option is fixed at 0, indicating that this option cannot be discarded by the receiver. In contrast, the Discardable Flag in the Load Balancing Option can be set to 1 if the option can be discarded by the receiver, allowing for more flexibility in handling load balancing options.

priority .
• Weight [uint16]: Carries the Weight of this instance. Large value means higher
probability to be chosen.

The IPv4 Endpoint Option is used by a SOME/IP-SD instance to signal the relevant
endpoint(s). Endpoints include the local IP address, the transport layer protocol (e.g.
UDP or TCP), and the port number of the sender. These ports are used for the events
and notification events as well.

Format of the IPv4 Endpoint Option shall be as fol-

• Length [uint16]: Shall be set to 0x0009.
• T ype [uint8]: Shall be set to 0x04.
• Discardable Flag [1 bit]: Shall be set to 0.

• Reserved [uint8]: Shall be set to 0x00.

user_input: How does the distinction between cat1 and cat2 interrupts in terms of interaction with the OS influence the support and configuration requirements for these interrupts as described in the OS's code generation and XML configuration?
reference: Cat1 interrupts, which are not allowed to interact with the OS data structures beyond enabling or disabling all interrupts, require minimal support from the OS. The OS's code generator and libraries do not need to abstract the interrupt from the hardware, and the XML configuration may or may not be used, depending on the target. In contrast, cat2 interrupts, which can make most OS calls, require more extensive support. The OS must generate the vector table and interrupt hardware manipulation code to manage the entry and exit of cat2 ISRs, and the XML configuration is used to capture all relevant information for these interrupts.
reference_contexts:

maps those steps onto the different BSW modules involved.

There are significant differences between cat1 and cat2 interrupts. These are 
summarized in the next table.

}

Configuration. This

The XML contains all the

cat1 interrupts. However, it 
may or may not be used, 
depending upon the target. 
The XML contains all the 
relevant information about 
a cat2 interrupts. This is 
then used to generate the 
vector table and interrupt 
hardware manipulation 
code to get into and out of

user_input: Given the specific signal transfer properties and unique IPDU structure required for some test cases in the communication database, how would these requirements impact the transmission behavior of the FlexRay frame when it contains stopped PDUs at the point in time they are triggered to be sent by the FlexRay Interface?
reference: If the FlexRay frame layout contains only stopped PDUs at the point in time they are triggered to be sent by the FlexRay Interface, no FlexRay Frame shall be transmitted, regardless of the specific signal transfer properties and unique IPDU structure required for the test cases in the communication database. This is because the transmission behavior is governed by the presence of started PDUs, which are necessary for the FlexRay Frame to be transmitted.

user_input: How does the implementation of the IPv6 Endpoint Option, as specified in the SOME/IP Service Discovery Protocol, influence the server's behavior in offering services over multicast and unicast during the Repetition and Main Phases?
reference: The IPv6 Endpoint Option, which includes the IPv6 address, transport protocol, and port number, is essential for the server to correctly configure and offer services over both multicast and unicast. During the Repetition Phase, the server offers services using multicast on the specified multicast address. In the Main Phase, the server responds to Find Service requests with unicast, using the details provided in the IPv6 Endpoint Option to establish the connection.
reference_contexts:
SOME/IP Service Discovery Protocol Specification
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0
[PRS_SOMEIPSD_00315]⌈ The Format of the IPv6 Endpoint Option shall be as fol-
lows:
• Length [uint16]: Shall be set to 0x0015.
• T ype [uint8]: Shall be set to 0x06.
• Discardable Flag [1 bit]: Shall be set to 0.
• Bit 1 to bit 7 are reserved and shall be 0.
• IPv6-Address [uint128]: Shall transport the unicast IP-Address as 16 Bytes.
• Reserved [uint8]: Shall be set to 0x00.
• T ransport Protocol (L4-Proto) [uint8]: Shall be set to the transport layer protocol
(ISO/OSI layer 4) based on the IANA/IETF types (0x06: TCP , 0x11: UDP).
• T ransport Protocol Port Number (L4-Port) [uint16]: Shall be set to the transport
layer port(e.g. 30490).
⌋(RS_SOMEIPSD_00006, RS_SOMEIPSD_00010)
[PRS_SOMEIPSD_00317]⌈

IPv4 SD Endpoint Option (receiving at least)
⌋(RS_SOMEIPSD_00025, RS_SOMEIPSD_00007)
[PRS_SOMEIPSD_00498]⌈ The following option types shall be implemented, if IPv6
is required:
• IPv6 Endpoint Option
• IPv6 Multicast Option
• Configuration Option
• IPv6 SD Endpoint Option (receiving at least)
⌋(RS_SOMEIPSD_00025, RS_SOMEIPSD_00007)
[PRS_SOMEIPSD_00500]⌈ The following behaviors/reactions shall be implemented
on the Server side:
• The Server shall offer services including the Initial Wait Phase, the Repetition
Phase, and the Main Phase depending on the configuration.
• The Server shall offer services using Multicast (Repetition Phase and Main
Phase) on the multicast address defined by SD_MUL TICAST_IP .
• The Server does not need to answer a Find Service in the Repetition Phase.
• The Server shall answer a Find Service in the Main Phase with an Offer Service
using Unicast (no optimization based on unicast flag).
• The Server shall send a Stop Offer Service when shutting down.
• The Server shall receive a Subscribe Eventgroup as well as a Stop Subscribe
Eventgroup and react according to this specification.
• The Server shall send a Subscribe Eventgroup Ack and Subscribe Eventgroup
Nack using unicast.
• The Server shall support controlling the sending (i.e. fan out) of SOME/IP event
messages based on the subscriptions of SOME/IP-SD. This might include send-
ing events based on Multicast.
• The Server shall support the triggering of initial SOME/IP event messages.
⌋(RS_SOMEIPSD_00013, RS_SOMEIPSD_00008, RS_SOMEIPSD_00014,
RS_SOMEIPSD_00015, RS_SOMEIPSD_00017, RS_SOMEIPSD_00025,
RS_SOMEIPSD_00007)
[PRS_SOMEIPSD_00501]⌈ The following behaviors/reactions shall be implemented
on the Client side:
• The Client shall find services using a Find Service entry and Multicast (on the
multicast address defined by SD_MUL TICAST_IP) only in the repetition phase.

user_input: How does the variation binding time defined in the system's variability (CONTEXT 1) influence the pre-compile time variability of dataReceivePointByArgument and dataReceivePointByValue in the AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0 standard (CONTEXT 2)?
reference: The variation binding time in the system's variability, which determines when the variability of the system is resolved, influences the pre-compile time variability of dataReceivePointByArgument and dataReceivePointByValue in the AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0 standard. Specifically, both dataReceivePointByArgument and dataReceivePointByValue are subject to variability with the latest binding time set to pre-compile time, ensuring that the conditional or variant existence of sender-receiver ports or data receive points is resolved before compilation.
reference_contexts:
Variability Variability of a system is its quality to describe a set of variants. These
variants are characterized by variant specific property settings and / or selections.
As an example, such a system property selection manifests itself in a particular
“receive port” for a connection.
This is implemented using the ≪atpVariation≫.
Variant A system variant is a concrete realization of a system, so that all its proper-
ties have been set respectively selected. The software system has no variability
anymore with respect to the binding time.
This is implemented using EvaluatedVariantSet.
Variation Binding A variant is the result of a variation binding process that resolves
the variability of the system by assigning particular values/selections to all the
system’s properties.
This is implemented by VariationPoint.
Variation Binding Time The variation binding time determines the step in the method-
ology at which the variability given by a set of variable properties is resolved.

user_input: How does the role of the ExteriorLightManager in managing the deactivation of exterior lights interact with the functionality of the ExteriorLightAdapter in calculating and applying brightness values?
reference: The ExteriorLightManager is responsible for the complete deactivation of exterior lights, while the ExteriorLightAdapter calculates and applies brightness values for the lights. The Adapter outputs a brightness value in percentage relative to the full brightness of the light, which is then used to control the light's intensity. However, the deactivation of a function, such as turning off a light completely, is managed by the ExteriorLightManager or FlashManager, ensuring that the Adapter focuses solely on dimming and not on the complete deactivation of the light.
reference_contexts:
Explanation of Application Interfaces of the Body and Comfort Domain 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.3.1 
40 of 98 Document ID 268: AUTOSAR_EXP_AIBodyAndComfort 
Exterior Light Manager
(ExtLiMgr) 
not not furtherfurther
brokenbroken downdown
The input EgyMngtSts1 may be used in ExteriorLightAdapter to dim ce rtain 
lights if en ergy management requests so. A complete deactivation of a function 
should already be taken into account only by ExteriorLightManager or FlashManag-
er. Note also that the output from ExteriorLightAdapter is a brightness value in 
percentage relative to the full brightness of the light. Thus ExteriorLightAdapter 
does not perform dimming due to over -voltage from po wer supply. This dimming is 
light type specific and has to be performed by the respective ExteriorLightActu-
ator components. 
 
For each light that is co ntrolled by ExteriorLightAdapter, the software comp o-
nent calculates a brightness value (in percentage relative to full brightness of the 
light). Thus, a value of 0% means that the light is switched off, and 100% means that 
the light has to be lit with full  brightness. Brightness values have been chosen since 
they are independent of the light source implementation (bulb or neon or LED or LED 
array or xenon or …). Furthermore, even lamps of the same type may have different 
voltages used for full brightness an d different response curves for different input vol t-
ages.  
 
For light sources that do not support dimming (such as some gas emitter lamps) or 
that support a limited set of brightness values (such as LED array that switch on 
more or less LEDs), a useful tra nslation from the different dimming factors to the le v-
els that can be set has to be defined and set by the actuator component.

user_input: How does the potential failure mode of wrong or missing initialization of the ADC hardware, as described in the data processing context, impact the safety measures required for the conversion of measured current in ECU25, as outlined in the safety use case example?
reference: Wrong or missing initialization of the ADC hardware can lead to unpredictable behavior, which directly impacts the safety measures required for the conversion of measured current in ECU25. To mitigate this, several safety mechanisms are recommended, including ECC for random hardware faults, CRC to detect modifications, duplication of source configuration data for comparison, hardware-supported protection mechanisms to prevent re-initialization, and watchdog management to ensure initialization occurs only once. These measures ensure the integrity and reliability of the ADC hardware, which is crucial for accurate current conversion.

user_input: Given the redefinition of standard C++ types within the ara::core namespace for the Adaptive Platform, what are the implications for using std::shared_ptr<T[]> in C++17 and later versions when implementing AUTOSAR modules?
reference: The redefinition of standard C++ types within the ara::core namespace for the Adaptive Platform implies that developers must be cautious when using std::shared_ptr<T[]> in C++17 and later versions. While std::shared_ptr<T[]> is available in C++17, moving a std::unique_ptr<T[]> into a std::shared_ptr<T> is no longer allowed, and such usage is considered non-compliant. Developers should prefer using containers like std::array or std::vector, or a smart pointer to a container, to manage arrays of objects, ensuring compliance with the rules and avoiding potential errors.
reference_contexts:
18.2 Advanced data types 
In addition to AUTOSAR-devised data types, which are mentioned in the previous 
section, the Adaptive Platform also contains a number of generic data types. 
 
Some of these types are already contained in the C++11 standard; however, types 
with almost identical behavior are re-defined within the ara::core namespace. The 
reason for this is that the memory allocation behavior of the std:: types is often

Note that corresponding features for std::shared_ptr are only available in C++17
(usage of std::shared_ptr<T[]> with C++11 and C++14 will lead to compilation
errors). The overloads for std::make_shared will only be available in C++20.
Furthermore, note that it is possible to create a smart pointer of single object type with
a custom deleter handling an array of objects. This is well behaving as long as this
smart pointer is actually managing an array of objects. However, such a use is error-
prone, since the smart pointer can be assigned a single object again in the reset
function; it may no longer be possible in C++17 (moving a std::unique_ptr<T[]>
into a std::shared_ptr<T> is no longer allowed); and it is superseded by better
alternatives in C++17 (availability of std:shared_ptr<T[]>). Therefore such usage
is considered not compliant to this rule.
In many cases, using a container such as std::array or std::vector or a smart
pointer to a container, e.g. std::shared_ptr<std::vector<T>>, is a better
alternative than a smart pointer to an array of objects.
Example
1 // $Id: A18-1-4.cpp 313638 2018-03-26 15:34:51Z jan.babst $
2 #include <memory>
3 class A
4 {
5 };
6 void F1()
7 {
8 // Create a dynamically allocated array of 10 objects of type A.
9 auto up1 = std::make_unique<A[]>(10); // Compliant
10
11 std::unique_ptr<A> up2{up1.release()}; // Non-compliant
12 }
13 void F2()
14 {
15 auto up1 = std::make_unique<A[]>(10); // Compliant
16
17 std::unique_ptr<A> up2;
18 up2.reset(up1.release()); // Non-compliant
19 }
20 void F3()
21 {
22 auto up = std::make_unique<A[]>(10); // Compliant
23
24 std::shared_ptr<A> sp{up.release()}; // Non-compliant
25 }

user_input: How does the initialization and activation process for the IPDU group AT_234_IpduGroup in the test ATS_COMINDEP_00235 compare with the process for the IPDU group AT_227_IpduGroup in the test ATS_COMFR_00228, particularly in terms of the timing and signal updates?
reference: In both tests, the IPDU groups are activated through a ModeSwitch request, which starts the respective IPDU groups. For AT_234_IpduGroup, the first TP transmission occurs after OffsetTime, followed by periodic transmissions every PeriodTime. The signal AT_234_Sg1 is initially set to AT_234_Sg1_Value_Init and is updated to AT_234_Sg1_Value_1 using Rte_Send(). For AT_227_IpduGroup, the IPDU is sent out after OffsetTime and then periodically every PeriodTime. The signal AT_227_Sg1 is initially set to AT_227_Sg1_Value_Init and is updated to AT_227_Sg1_Value_1 using Rte_Write(), with the update bit set to 1 during the first send and reset to 0 thereafter.

user_input: How does the Watchdog Manager's supervision of Supervised Entities, as described in the logical supervision of the execution sequence, influence the way CDD runnables must interact with the Watchdog Manager through the Rte defined ports?
reference: The Watchdog Manager supervises the execution of Supervised Entities, which can include CDD runnables, to ensure they follow the correct execution sequence. To facilitate this supervision, CDD runnables must interact with the Watchdog Manager through Rte defined ports, rather than directly. The Rte propagates checkpoint information from the Supervised Entities to the Watchdog Manager and informs the CDD about changes in the supervision status. This interaction ensures that the Watchdog Manager can effectively monitor and manage the execution sequence of the CDD runnables, maintaining the integrity of the application.
reference_contexts:
Description 
Logical Supervision of the execution sequence of a program enables the detection of 
errors that cause a divergence from the valid program sequence during the error -free 
execution of the application. An incorrect program flow occurs if one or more program 
instructions are processed either in an i ncorrect sequence or not even processed at 
all. 
 
The  Watchdog Manager supervises the execution of Application Software in an 
AUTOSAR ECU. The logical units of supervision are called  Supervised Entities. 
There is no fixed relationship between Supervised Ent ities and the architectural 
building blocks in AUTOSAR. Typically a Supervised Entity may represent one SW -
Cs or a Runnable within an SW -C, a BSW module or CDD depending on the choice 
of the developer.  
                                            
26 [ISO 26262-6, Annex D] D.2.2 Timing and execution

Interfacing with Watchdog Stack 
The Watchdog manager may supervise the execution of one or more runnables of a 
CDD as supervised entities. The watchdog manager shall be configured and CDD 
runnable shall call Watchdog API as described in the document [12] Watchdog 
Manager.    
 
The Watchdog Manager is the exclusive access point to the watchdog stack. 
 
CDD should not interact directly with the watchdog manager but through the Rte 
defined ports. 
 
Usually, the Rte is responsible for propagating Checkpoint information from 
Supervised Entities in CDD to the Watchdog Manager module. The Watchdog 
Manager module uses the services of the Runtime Environment to inform CDD about 
changes in the supervision status. 
 
To control the state-dependent behavior of CDD, the Rte provides the mechanism of 
mode ports. A mode manager can switch between different modes that are defined in 
the mode port. The CDD that connects to the mode port can use the mode 
information in two ways: 
 The CDD can query the current mode via the mode port. 
 The CDD can declare Runnables that are started or stopped by the Rte 
because of mode changes. 
 
In case of failure, the Watchdog Manager may inform the CDD Supervised Entity 
about supervision failures via the Rte Mode mechanism. The CDD Supervised Entity 
may then take its actions to recover from that failure. 
7.3.5

user_input: How does the change in the signal TMC from FALSE to TRUE in the AT_220_SgGr1 group affect the periodicity of the AT_220_Ipdu transmissions compared to the initial transmission conditions described for AT_261_Sg1?
reference: When the signal TMC in the AT_220_SgGr1 group changes from FALSE to TRUE, the periodicity of the AT_220_Ipdu transmissions changes from being sent every TxModeFalse Period to being sent immediately and then every TxModeTrue Period. In contrast, the initial transmission conditions for AT_261_Sg1 specify that the signal is sent out only once immediately when its value changes from the initial value to a new value.

user_input: How does the integration of Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs, as described in the introduction, impact the problems of mapping ports and interfaces to predefined communication messages in the interaction with Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs?
reference: The integration of Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs, which includes ECUs not developed according to AUTOSAR mechanisms, existing ECUs in a system, and intelligent sensors/actuators without AUTOSAR VFB/RTE, introduces the problem of mapping ports and interfaces to predefined communication messages. This mapping is necessary to route communication through gateways, but it results in unconnected ports of AUTOSAR SW-Components and hidden communication load, complicating the interaction between AUTOSAR and Non-AUTOSAR components.
reference_contexts:
Virtual Functional Bus 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
 
101 of 104 Document ID 056: AUTOSAR_EXP_VFB 
12  Interaction with Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs 
 12.1 Introduction 
This section describes the interaction with Non -AUTOSAR-ECUs on VFB level. This 
kind of interaction is e.g. necessary to provide a migration path. 
Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs are: 
ECUs that have not been developed according to AUTOSAR mechanisms. This is useful 
for e.g.: 
o Integration of an AUTOSAR ECU into an already existing system of ECUs 
o Connect system of AUTOSAR ECUs to already existing system of ECUs 
o Re-use already existing ECU in system of AUTOSAR ECUs 
ECUs that have been developed according to AUTOSAR mechanisms  once, but stay 
unchanged now. This is useful for e.g.: 
o Reuse strategies (taking over of complete unchangeable AUTOSAR (!!!) 
ECUs) 
Intelligent ('Smart') Sensors/Actuators with an ECU which do not implement the 
AUTOSAR VFB / AUTOSAR RTE. This is useful for e.g.: 
o Using Commercial of the shelf LIN nodes. 
Interaction of A UTOSAR SW-C with non AUTOSAR software within one ECU is not 
analyzed in this document.

12.2 Problems of interaction 
The following problems will arise from the interaction with Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs: 
 
 Interaction with interfaces of applications on Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs: 
 Ports/Interfaces have to be mapped to pre -defined communication messages 
(possible to be routed through gateway) 
 Non-AUTOSAR-SW-Components are currently not modeled at VFB level 
o  Unconnected ports of AUTOSAR-SW-Components 
o  Hidden communication load 
 Client-Server not supported in old systems. 
 
 Interaction/support of services implemented on Non-AUTOSAR ECUs 
 Old services/protocols have to be supported in parallel, to enable interoperab ility, e.g. 
Network Management.  
 Additional services supported by communication system (e.g. bus sleep/bus 
wake-up). 
 LIN nodes inherently are not affected because it is using the master slave paradigm  
o  services/protocols have to be managed and implemented in any case 
by master node (in this case AUTOSAR ECU) 
o  Required configuration data available in node capability file (NCF) 
 
Problem of support of enhanced services/protocols (e.g. Network Management, 
Diagnosis (connection to AUTOSAR SW-C), Transport Protocol Layer, ...)

user_input: How does the memory partitioning mechanism described in the AUTOSAR OS specification interact with the requirements for generating glue code when ASIL modules call each other across different ASIL partitions?
reference: The memory partitioning mechanism in the AUTOSAR OS ensures that OS-Applications are isolated from each other, preventing write access to private data sections and stacks. When ASIL modules call each other across different ASIL partitions, the requirement for generating glue code arises to manage these interactions. The glue code must handle the transition between partitions, ensuring that the calling and called modules adhere to the memory protection requirements. This may involve static or dynamically generated glue code, depending on the specific configuration and the need to encapsulate calls to integrator code or manage generic interfaces using function pointers.
reference_contexts:
Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
13 of 96 Document ID 664: AUTOSAR_TR_OverviewOfFunctionalSafetyMeasuresInAUTOSAR 
 
2.1.2.4  Memory Partitioning within Application Software 
Application Software in an AUTOSAR ECU can consist of safety -related and non -
safety-related Software Component s.  Freedom from interference between Software 
Components with different ASIL ratings shall be ensured according to the 
requirements of ISO 262629. 
 
The AUTOSAR Operating System provides freedom from interference for memory -
related faults by placing OS -Applications into separate memory regions. This 
mechanism is called Memory Partitioning. OS -Applications are protected from each 
other, as code executing in the Memory Partition of one OS -Application cannot 
modify other memory regions. The corresponding requi rements from the AUTOSAR 
OS specification are presented in Table 1. 
 
Req. ID Requirement Text 
[SWS_Os_ 
00207] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to the OS -
Application’s private data sections from other non -trusted OS -
Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00355] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to all private 
stacks of Tasks/Category 2 ISRs of an OS -Application from other non -
trusted OS-Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00356] 
The Operating System module shall pre vent write access to all private 
data sections of a Task/Category 2 ISR of an OS -Application from other 
non-trusted OS-Applications. 
Table 1:

3.2.3.3 ASIL calls ASIL 
The case of an ASIL to ASIL call can be seen as a combination of 3.2.3.2 and 
3.2.3.1. Also here a generic glue code might be needed if the modules are not placed 
in the same ASIL partition. In this case either the caller or the callee have to provide 
this glue code. In an ASIL system the glue code is normally provided by those 
modules which have the higher ASIL. The glue code can be created statically or can 
be generated.  
 
For the generation of the glue code the following limitations exist: 
 
 Calls to integrator code: A configuration via SchM_Call() is not possible since the 
integrator code does not belong to any BSW module and does not have any 
configuration (EcuConfiguration) and module (BSWDT) information which could 
be used. In such cases 
o Either a static glue code has to be used to encapsulate calls from/to 
integrator code and the integrator code might need small changes, e.g. 
changing the name of the called function to avoid name clashes.  
o or offer vendor specific configuration parameter which holds per callout a 
reference to the OsApplication where the integration code is placed. 
 
 If we know only the address of the callee (this can happen if the interface is 
generic and function pointers are used for the call, e.g. in the PDU Router) we 
need a dedicated vendor specific configuration parameter for the ASIL module 
which provides the information in which partition the callee is located.

user_input: How does the timeout detection mechanism described in the first context interact with the counter initialization and incrementation rules specified in E2E Profile 22?
reference: The timeout detection mechanism utilizes the counter to identify communication losses and timeouts. In E2E Profile 22, the counter is initialized to 0 and incremented by 1 for each subsequent transmission, restarting at 0 after reaching 0x0F. On the receiver side, the counter is evaluated against the previous counter value to detect repetitions, normal increments, or excessive increments. If the counter does not increment as expected, indicating no new data or too many data losses, the receiver can infer a timeout or communication failure.
reference_contexts:
Timeout detection
The previously mentioned mechanisms (CRC, Counter, Data ID, Length) enable to
check the validity of received data element, when the receiver is executed indepen-
dently from the data transmission, i.e. when receiver is not blocked waiting for Data
Elements or respectively I-PDUs, but instead if the receiver reads the currently avail-
able data (i.e. checks if new data is available). Then, by means of the counter, the
receiver can detect loss of communication and timeouts.

6.10.2 Counter
In E2E Profile 22, the counter is initialized, incremented, reset and checked by E2E
profile check and protect functions. The counter is not manipulated or used by the
caller of the E2E Supervision. .
[PRS_E2E_00523]⌈ In E2E Profile 22, on the sender side, for the first transmission re-
quest of a data element the counter shall be initialized with 0 and shall be incremented
by 1 for every subsequent send request. When the counter reaches the maximum
value (0x0F), then it shall restart with 0 for the next send request. ⌋(RS_E2E_08539)
Note that the counter value 0x0F is not reserved as a special invalid value.
In E2E Profile 22, on the receiver side, by evaluating the counter of received data
against the counter of previously received data, the following is detected:
1. Repetition:
a. no new data has arrived since last invocation of E2E Supervision check func-
tion,
b. the data is repeated
2. OK:
a. counter is incremented by one (i.e. no data lost),
b. counter is incremented more than by one, but still within allowed limits (i.e.
some data lost),
3. Error: a. counter is incremented more than allowed (i.e. too many data lost).
Case 1 corresponds to the failed alive counter check, and case 3 correspond to failed
sequence counter check.
The above requirements are specified in more details by the UML diagrams in the
following document sections.

user_input: How does the local event cache mechanism described in the binding implementation interact with the on-change-notification feature provided by the ara::com API when updating field values?
reference: When a service implementer updates a field value using the Update method, the binding implementation makes a serialized copy of the data. If on-change-notification is configured for the field, the binding implementation triggers notifications to all subscribed entities. These notifications are sent to the local event caches of the respective ara::com proxies, ensuring that the cached data is updated to reflect the new field value.

 6.2.3.4 Buffering Strategies

At this point it surely makes sense to talk about reasonable buffering strate-
gies for binding implementations. So this entire subsection is mainly of interest for
an AP product vendor/binding implementer.
The following figure sketches a simple deployment, where we have a service providing

As you can see in the picture both proxies have a local event cache. This is the cache,

is apparently outside the process space of the service implementation — the picture

 Listing 6.25: Skeleton side UpdateRate Class
The using directive — again as in the Event Class and on the Proxy side — just intro-
duces the common name FieldType for the concrete data type of the field.
We provide an Update method by which the service implementer can update the cur-
rent value of the field.
It is very similar to the simple/first variant of the Send method of the event class: The
field data has been allocated somewhere by the service application developer and is
given via reference to the binding implementation of Update. After the call to Update
returns, the data might be removed/altered on the caller side.
The binding implementation will make a (typically serialized) copy in the call.
In case “on-change-notification” is configured for the field, notifications to subscribers
of this field will be triggered by the binding implementation in the course of the Update
call.
 6.3.6.1 Registering Getters
The RegisterGetHandler method provides the possibility to register a method im-
plementation by the service implementer, which gets then called by the binding imple-
mentation on an incoming Get() call from any proxy instance.
The RegisterGetHandler method in the generated skeleton does only exist in case
availability of “field getter” has been configured for the field in the IDL!
Registration of such a “GetHandler” is fully optional! T ypically there is no need for a
service implementer to provide such a handler. The binding implementation always has
access to the latest value, which has been set via Update. So any incoming Get()
call can be served by the Communication Management implementation standalone.

user_input: Given the requirement that data integrity shall be checked using ECC as specified in SRS_FlsTst_14202, how does this interact with the AUTOSAR convention that 'SHALL' indicates an absolute requirement? What are the implications for the design and implementation of the Flash Test module?
reference: The requirement that data integrity shall be checked using ECC, as specified in SRS_FlsTst_14202, is an absolute requirement according to the AUTOSAR convention that 'SHALL' indicates an absolute requirement. This means that the design and implementation of the Flash Test module must include an algorithm to actively test data integrity using ECC, which must be capable of detecting and reporting single, double, and triple-bit failures in a 16-bit word. The implementation must also report a DEM error in case of any failure, ensuring robust data integrity checks.

user_input: How does the use of End-2-End Profiles in AUTOSAR, specifically the timeout detection mechanism, ensure compliance with the requirement RS_BRF_00113 for detecting signal time-outs in periodic signals?
reference: The End-2-End Profiles in AUTOSAR include a timeout detection mechanism, which ensures that periodic signals are exchanged within a defined time interval. If a message is not received within this interval, it is considered a timeout, and this can be used to detect errors in the communication system, such as the loss of messages. This mechanism aligns with the requirement RS_BRF_00113, which mandates that AUTOSAR must provide a way to detect if periodic signals are not exchanged within a specified time frame.
reference_contexts:
Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
40 of 96 Document ID 664: AUTOSAR_TR_OverviewOfFunctionalSafetyMeasuresInAUTOSAR 
 2.4.2.1 End-2-End Profiles 
AUTOSAR specifies a set of standardized and configurable End-2-End profiles, 
which implement a set of protection mechanisms and specify the data format for the 
attached End-2-End header. 
An End-2-End Profile uses a subset of the following data protection mechanisms:34 
1. CRC Checksum, provided by the CRC library; 
2. Sequence Counter incremented at every transmission request, the value is 
checked at receiver side for correct incrementation; 
3. Alive Counter incremented at every transmission request, the value checked at 
the receiver side if it changes at all, but correct incrementation is not checked 
4. A specific ID for every port data element sent over a port (global to system, where 
the system may contain potentially several ECUs). 
5. Timeout detection: Receiver communication timeout and Sender 
acknowledgement timeout  
 
 Three End-2-End Profiles are specified in the AUTOSAR Standard, Profile 1 with two 
variants, End-2-End Profile 2 and End -2-End Profile 4. Upcoming releases will also 
specify Profiles 5 and 6. 
Only the standardized End -2-End profiles shall be used, non -standard End -2-End 
Profile configurations may only be used in special cases, such as for legacy software.

4.11.8 [RS_BRF_00113] AUTOSAR shall detect signal time-outs 
⌈  
Type: Valid 
Description: AUTOSAR shall provide a mechanism that detects if periodic signals are not 
exchanged within a defined time interval (time-out). This can be used for 
detecting errors in the communication system (loss of messages). If a 
message is coming too late, even if it is correct (correct sequence number, 
checksum etc.), it shall be considered as an error. The actual handling shall

user_input: How does the behavior of updating a signal with the same value in Context 1 compare to the behavior of updating a GroupSignal with the initial value in Context 2, and what are the implications for the I-PDU send out period in both scenarios?
reference: In both Context 1 and Context 2, updating a signal or GroupSignal with the same value results in no change to the I-PDU send out period. Specifically, in Context 1, updating a signal with the same value Value_1 does not trigger an I-PDU send out event, and the I-PDU send out period remains unchanged. Similarly, in Context 2, updating a GroupSignal with its initial value also does not change the I-PDU send out period, and no additional I-PDU send out event is triggered. This consistent behavior ensures that redundant updates do not affect the periodic communication schedule on the FlexRay bus.

user_input: How does the requirement for access serialization across cores in multi-core environments impact the design of reentrant shared code in AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules, particularly in ensuring concurrency safety?
reference: In multi-core environments, access serialization across cores is essential to prevent concurrent access to shared resources. This requirement impacts the design of reentrant shared code in AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules by necessitating that such code is concurrency-safe. Specifically, shared code must ensure reentrancy, which involves avoiding the use of static and global variables, guarding these variables with blocking mechanisms, and using dynamic stack variables. These measures are crucial to prevent conflicts when the same code is executed simultaneously on multiple cores.
reference_contexts:
Constraints 
To realize the concept further constraints are defined to prevent inefficient and multi-
core blocking implementations. In this sense it is especially important to consider that 
it is not sufficient anymore to implement exclusive areas on a single core but to 
additionally ensure an access serialization in case resources need to be shared 
across several partitions distributed to several cores. 
 
1.  Access Serialization on a single core: For single-core systems concurrency 
problems are well understood and mitigated by exclusive areas, which limit 
concurrent access to one process at a time. This is typically done by locking 
interrupts, employing OS resources or creating a non-pre-emptive scheduling. 
This effectively means access-serialization of the different processes. 
2.  Access Serialization across cores: Since exclusive areas only have a core-
wide scope, they are not sufficient to prevent concurrent access in multi-core 
environments. But as soon as it is required to access the same resource (e.g. 
by access to the service API’s, processing of the ISRs and main-functions) it is 
required to introduce cross-core means. Besides the usage of atomic 
resources, the worst – because blocking – one would be the introduction of a

Type: Valid 
Description: All AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules shall export only that kind of 
information in their correspondent header-files explicitly needed by other 
modules. 
Rationale: Prevent other modules accessing functionality and data that is ‘none of their 
business’.   
Use Case: The NVRAM Manager shall not know all processor registers because 
someone has included the processor register file in another header file used 
by the NVRAM manager. 
Dependencies: -- 
Supporting Material: -- 
⌋(RS_BRF_02024) 
5.2.3.5.3  [SRS_BSW_00328] All AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules shall avoid 
the duplication of code 
⌈ 
Type: Valid 
Description: All AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules should avoid the duplication of code. 
Rationale: Avoid bugs during maintenance 
Use Case: A module contains 4 code segments which are equal. During maintenance of 
the module 3 of them have been updated, 1 has been forgotten  BUG. 
Dependencies: -- 
Supporting Material: -- 
⌋(RS_BRF_02072,RS_BRF_02112,RS_BRF_02032) 
5.2.3.5.4 [SRS_BSW_00312] Shared code shall be reentrant  
⌈ 
Type: Valid 
Description: All AUTOSAR Basic Software Modules implementing shared code shall 
ensure reentrancy if code is exposed to preemptive or parallel environments. 
For multi-core systems, reentrancy shall be ensured for unrestricted 
concurrent execution of that service on several cores (concurrency safety). 
Rationale: Shared code eases functional composition, reusability, code size reduction 
and maintainability. As a drawback, shared code shall be implemented 
reentrant if it is used in preemptive environments or on multiple partitions in 
parallel. Please note that an implementation that is reentrant on single core 
systems might not be concurrency safe when used in a Multi-Core 
environment. 
Use Case: A subroutine or function is reentrant if a single copy of the routine can be 
called from several task contexts simultaneously without conflict. Use the 
following reentrancy techniques: 
 
- Avoid use of static and/or global variables 
- Guard static and/or global variables using blocking mechanisms 
- Use dynamic stack variables  
Dependencies: -- 
Supporting Material: --

user_input: Given that a transformer must return a hard error when it cannot generate a valid output (SWS_Xfrm_00027) and that the RTE hands over a NULL pointer to the transformer if it cannot provide input data (from the transformer parameters description), what is the expected behavior of a transformer when it receives a NULL pointer as input, and how should it handle this situation according to the error handling specifications?
reference: When a transformer receives a NULL pointer as input, it indicates that the RTE could not provide the required input data. According to the error handling specifications, if a transformer cannot generate a valid output, it must return a hard error. Therefore, the transformer should return a hard error (a value in the range 0x80 - 0xFF) and leave the output buffer unchanged.
reference_contexts:
Soft Errors and Hard Errors. If a
transformer returns a soft error, the Rte continues with the execution of the transformer
chain. If a transformer returns a hard error,the Rte aborts the execution of the trans-
former chain because the error was so severe that there are no meaningful data for the
next transformer in the chain.
The value range of errors is divided:
• 0x00: Success
• 0x01 - 0x7F: Soft Errors
• 0x80 - 0xFF: Hard Errors

user_input: How does the E2E_P02Protect() function's process of updating the Counter and calculating the CRC, as specified in E2E Profile 2, ensure the detection of masquerade and incorrect addressing faults as described in the E2E mechanisms?
reference: The E2E_P02Protect() function updates the Counter and calculates the CRC over the data buffer extended with the Data ID. The Counter ensures the detection of repetition, loss, insertion, and incorrect sequence faults. The Data ID, which is linked to the Counter value, along with the CRC, ensures the detection of masquerade and incorrect addressing faults by verifying the integrity and origin of the data. The unique Data ID list and the CRC computation provide a mechanism to detect corruption and asymmetric information, thus ensuring the reliability of the communication.
reference_contexts:
E2E Protocol Specification
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0
[PRS_E2E_00130]⌈ In E2E Profile 2, the E2E_P02Protect() function shall update the
Counter (i.e. low nibble (Bit 0...Bit 3) of Data byte 1) in the data buffer (Data) after
incrementing the Counter.⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
The specific Data ID used for this send request is then determined from a DataIDList[]
depending on the value of the Counter (Counter is used as an index to select the Data
ID from DataIDList[]). The DataIDList[] is defined in E2E_P02ConfigT ype.
[PRS_E2E_00132] ⌈ In E2E Profile 2, after determining the specific Data ID,
the E2E_P02Protect() function shall calculate the CRC over Data[1], Data[2], ...
Data[Config->DataLength/8-1] of the data buffer (Data) extended with the Data ID. ⌋
(RS_E2E_08528)
[PRS_E2E_00133] ⌈ In E2E Profile 2, the E2E_P02Protect() function shall up-
date the CRC (i.e. Data[0]) in the data buffer (Data) after computing the CRC. ⌋
(RS_E2E_08528)
The specific Data ID itself is not transmitted on the bus. It is just a virtual message key
used for the CRC calculation.

Control field Description
Counter 4 bits. (explicitly sent)
CRC 8 bits, polynomial in normal form 0x2F (Autosar notation), pro-
vided by CRC library . (explicitly sent)
Data ID List 16 8 bits values, linked to Counter value. Effectively 16 different
values, one for each counter value. The Data ID List must be
unique system-wide.

bases and timeout moni-
toring using E2E-Library 5
Loss, delay , blocking

CRC Corruption, asymmetric information 6

user_input: How does the Runtime Environment (RTE) ensure that a component's implementation can access and manipulate the data-elements and ports defined in its composition type, as referenced in AUTOSAR XML?
reference: The Runtime Environment (RTE) ensures that a component's implementation can access and manipulate the data-elements and ports defined in its composition type by providing appropriate mechanisms. These mechanisms allow the component to provide values for data-elements in its PPorts, read/consume values for data-elements in its RPorts, and invoke operations provided by other components through its RPorts. In AUTOSAR XML, instance references are used to link the component prototype to the specific port definitions in the composition type, enabling the RTE to correctly map and manage these interactions.
reference_contexts:
The VFB is a system modeling and communication concept, which allows 
components to be distributed in a network of ECUs.  The interaction possibilities 
between a component and other components are described through the component's 
ports and their associated interfaces , which define the operations, data -elements, 
mode-groups or calibration parameters  that are provided or required by the 
component.  Through the same communication mechanisms, the component  can 
interact with standardized AUTOSAR services (available on each properly configured 
AUTOSAR ECU) or the ECU -specific IO capabilities  (available on the specific ECU 
on which the appropriate hardware is present and to which the correct devices are 
connected). 
However, i mplementations of components need access to additional resources, 
mainly memory (the component’s implementation typically needs memory to maintain 
its internal state) and CPU -power (the component’s implementation contains code 
that must be executed acc ording to a certain timing schedule  or in response to 
certain events). 
As these scheduling issues are closely linked to the communication needs of the 
component, the RTE must provide both aspects.  Therefore, the RTE must provide a 
complete environment for the component, including: 
 Appropriate mechanisms through which the component’s implementation (for 
example in a programming language like “C”) can: 
o Provide values for data-elements in the component’s PPorts 
o Read/Consume values for data-elements in the component’s RPorts 
o Access the component’s calibration parameters 
o Provide implementations for the operations in the component’s PPorts 
o Invoke operations provided by other components through the 
component’s RPorts 
o Etc.

Instance References 
AUTOSAR XML allows to reference elements from the type definition for a particular 
instance of the type using instance references. E.g. component prototypes do not 
define ports, but only reference their composition type, which defines ports. If it is 
required to reference this port, a reference to the context element is needed. The 
reference contains the instance and the target element. For a port, the instance is the 
component prototype, and the target element is the port definition in the composition 
type. The following example defines a reference to the port WipgSpdIntlFromHmi at 
the component prototype 
/AUTOSAR/AISpecification/SwComponentTypes_Example/WiprWshr/WiprWshrMgr , which is 
defined in the composition type 
/AUTOSAR/AISpecification/SwComponentTypes_Example/WiprWshrMgr. 
 
<DELEGATION-SW-CONNECTOR> 
 <SHORT-NAME>WipgSpdIntlFromHmiToWipgSpdIntlFromHmiOfWiprWshrMgr</SHORT-
NAME> 
    <INNER-PORT-IREF> 
  <R-PORT-IN-COMPOSITION-INSTANCE-REF>
